scriptId: cs_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
historyId: h_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use

memories.content:
- User background: on workdays average 4–5 hours on the device, mostly in the evening; weekends higher; goal is ~1 hour on weekdays; asks if achievable in a few weeks.

- Assistant conclusion: It is achievable but ambitious; reducing from 4–5 hours to ~1 hour is a ~75–80% reduction; with focused effort, most people can reach near that in 4–6 weeks; 3–4 weeks possible but harder to sustain.

- 4-week progression:
  - Week 1: ~3–3.75 hours (≈25% cut)
  - Week 2: ~2–2.8 hours
  - Week 3: ~1.5–2 hours
  - Week 4: ~1 hour and sustainable habits

- High-impact tactics:
  - Set and enforce daily app limits
  - Introduce an evening cutoff (gradually earlier)
  - Make feeds harder to reach (move apps off home screen, log out, use blockers)
  - Replace evening scrolling with alternatives (reading, walks, shows, hobby)
  - Track daily minutes and one simple metric

- Weekend plan: weekends tougher; set a separate cap (2–3 hours) or schedule two short social windows

- Offer: can turn progression into exact limits and a short evening routine starting tomorrow

- A user asked how to approach notification management without turning everything off.
- The response provides a step-by-step method to reduce notification noise while preserving important alerts:
  - Quick audit (10–15 minutes) to identify the top 5 most

- User asked for reliable built-in features or free apps to enforce phone usage limits and which are hardest to beat.
- Response identifies built-in tools as most reliable: iPhone Screen Time and Android Digital Wellbeing.
  - iPhone: use App Limits for social apps + Block at End of Limit; schedule Downtime or a Focus; set a Screen Time

The user asked for a polite way to signal stepping back from quick replies without seeming rude; the response provided three principles (set expectations with slower replies and a predictable window, provide a clear urgent channel for time-sensitive matters, be concise and friendly) and ready-to-use scripts for different relationships (short one-liners for casual friends, closer friends/family, and acquaintances/groups), a longer version for frequent contacts, group-chat notes, work/semi-formal wording, profile/status and auto-reply ideas, politeness extras and consistency tips, plus a quick tactic to post the brief line

User asked how to preserve productive work/networking use of social apps while reducing passive scrolling. Assistant provided a ten-point plan of practical tactics: separate work from personal use with defined work windows and, if possible, different endpoints; configure notifications and use Focus modes to limit feed alerts; mute/unfollow algorithmic suggestions and disable autoplay; batch networking tasks into 2–3 short daily windows; use direct-message workflows and saved replies; bookmark content for later review with a read-later approach; place networking apps in a deliberate workflow with folders or extra steps (e.g., log out or re-authenticate); leverage desktop for deep work with feed-blocking tools; measure productive outputs (messages replied, introductions, follow-ups) rather than time spent; maintain a low-anxiety safety valve with urgent-contacts rules or a single daily check; and provided an example daily routine (morning inbox triage, midday networking window, evening scheduled session). Also offered to tailor a 1-page workflow to the user's specific apps and preferred platform.

User asked for physical adjustments at home to reduce automatic phone reaches. Response states physical changes are one of the most reliable methods and provides practical, low-friction adjustments categorized as follows:

- Charging & parking habits

User asked how to stay available for one emergency contact while limiting all other notifications. Assistant provided a detailed, platform-specific guide: for iPhone, use Focus/Do Not Disturb with the emergency contact allowed, enable Repeated Calls, use Emergency Bypass for that contact, and apply tweaks like turning off lock-screen previews and using a unique ringtone; for Android, use Do Not Disturb with exceptions for the emergency contact (e.g., Starred/Favorite) and enable Repeat Caller, plus per-app notification overrides
